tweets.text:
Contributed to this great piece
Here’s your TL;DR ↓

• Study scope: 71,697 buyers; $91.3B gross acquisitions across ten tokenised dollar-yield products; tokenised RWAs up 179% to $33.8B by July 2026.

• No TradFi flow in the attributable pool: of $12.4B we could attribute to buyer types, 66% was protocol/DAO treasuries and zero dollars were traced to pensions, asset managers, or banks.

• Extreme concentration: wallets writing checks ≥ $1M are ~4% of buyers but account for ~93% of acquired notional.

• Largest institutional tickets go to credit-like products: @centrifuge JAAA median institutional cheque ≈ $29.1M, ~3x the next-largest product.

• USDC is the dominant settlement rail: ~80% ($17.4B) of acquisitions used USDC; USDT accounts for ~$4.4B (mostly via syrupUSDT).

• Buyer base is fresh capital: median first activity dates to mid-2024 (retail ≈ Mar‑2024; institutional ≈ Jul‑2024); few wallets predate 2021.

• Primary subscription dominates: 93–100% of acquisitions are primary mints; genuine DEX entry is 0–6% across products except @ethena sUSDe at 55% DEX-sourced.

• Leverage is limited and concentrated: most products lack lending markets; mF-ONE peaked near 42% borrowed vs notional then unwound to ~8%; AA_FalconXUSDC peaked ~29% and was ~25% outstanding on July 7, 2026.

• Retention skews to credit/treasury: TradFi-like credit funds retain ~68% of buyers at one year; tokenised treasuries ~60%; crypto-native carry/credit products churn to ~28% by year-end.

• Operating hours skew non‑US: EMEA ~42%, APAC ~40%, Americas ~18% by buyer activity window.

• Unattributed institutional capital largely CEX/DeFi-funded after two hops: of $5.17B otherwise-unattributed, ~40% traces to CEX funding (Binance ≈ $1.12B noted), ~38% DeFi-native, ~22% unidentified.

• Net composite APY demanded by large allocators rises with credit-likeness: ≈2.3% on synthetic-dollar carry up to ≈7.4% on gated private-credit funds.

• Retail is headcount, not capital: @Ondo USDY is 95.6% retail by buyer count yet those wallets hold under 1% of USDY value; Ethena sUSDe concentrates most retail presence but retail remains a minority of capital.

• Secondary liquidity is the biggest composability gap: without DEX/secondary depth, RWAs remain subscription products and rarely function as continuous collateral or composable assets.

The onchain demand profile looks like freshly formed crypto-native treasuries, exchanges and funds spinning up purpose-built wallets in 2024 and buying primarily at issuance via USDC rails.

Where lending markets and DEX liquidity exist, holders post collateral and looping creates leverage (seen in mF-ONE and AA_FalconX); where markets don’t exist, capital sits in spot earning base yield.

These dynamics explain concentration, the preference for primary issuance, and why retail shows up in counts but not capital share.

Oh yeah, this pain is real.

My thinking is that 95% of the existing neobanks will either repeat the fate of @Cypher_HQ_ or will just wind down & I will add them to my RIP list – because their math is not matching.

Yeah, it's relatively cheap to start a crypto card ($150K of initial investment) but you have monthly maintenance costs & users don't earn you as much as you expected; infra is eating up 80% of the profits made.

Each user acquired through paid is VERY expensive.
Based on the latest cases we see at @GREEND0TS it's ~$70–80 per KYC now (influence marketing / X ads / Google etc).

So it's logical that they make up referral terms that end up being ultra conditional. They need to make money somewhere and they make it by rugging their referrers.

Not a viable long-term plan, ngl.

not that anyone cares, but my opinion is that this is a great idea

in private conversations i’ve veen saying for a while that i believe bitcoin treasury companies, etf issuers, exchanges, etc should assume a leadership role in bitcoin development

no one else was going to do it. the “reddit troll who is also a part-time software engineer” software development model for bitcoin stopped working roughly 10 years ago

hilariously the bip110tards are crying about this, but only a few weeks ago they crucified gloria for daring to contribute to bitcoin while (allegedly) not owning any btc

the only logical conclusion would be that bitcoin protocol development should therefore be driven by the largest holders. it makes perfect sense and is the best available way to align incentives

that is not to say that they will immediately do a good job. they’re just starting out. they’ll probably make mistakes. but they have the incentives to course-correct over time, and that’s all you can ask for

for most of the last decade, a group of reddit trolls convinced bitcoin holders that bitcoin protocol developers shouldn’t care about bitcoin price. that they should be “above it”. that is stupid. that approach failed. 100% of the controversy around bitcoin code stewardship has been due to that misalignment

the choice of words here is also very telling. the corporations view the role of the software devs they fund to be “securing the network”. not developing it. they are not decision makers. they are janitors. good.

tweets.text:
$INTC up 11% on June earnings. 

• My bottom line. What's going on is much bigger than INTC. Results are a sign that we are still much early in AI than many think.

• I think of Intel CPUs as an accessory to the AI buildout. Its business is getting pulled along by NVDA.

• To put it into perspective, $NVDA’s GPU business is 13x bigger than Intels most exciting segment, DCAI, with NVDA expected to grow at 96% in July. Intel DCAI just printed 59% growth. Intel is more successful at beating the law of large numbers. 

• Intel is great and there are better AI companies to own to invest in AI. Deepwater does not own shares.

• The guide ups have been getting larger over the past two quarters. Assuming they hit the high end of guidance, they guided Sep revenue up 11% this quarter, up 13% in Mar-25, up 1% in Dec-25, and up 3% in Sep-25.

• This is important because it’s a sign analysts are having a hard time keeping up with the true growth. That means the Street still doesn’t get the big picture of what’s going on. What is going on is we are earlier than most believe in AI infrastructure buildout.

• Margins are likely going to be flat in September, at 42%. That’s a good sign vs what we say from GOOG last night.

• NVDA is 13x bigger and almost 2x.Valuation: Intel trades at 61x CY27 EPS. NVDA at 17x, and AMD at 40x.

More to come on the call.

tweets.text:
Technically, the deal has not officially collapsed yet. But in reality, it is over. The Prime Minister and the Minister of Defense have already made it clear that the transaction will not be approved for national security reasons.

The fact that a company was willing to pay around $35 per share, with the possibility that other bidders could have offered even more, says a lot about $ZIM ’s value.

When an acquirer is willing to pay that kind of price, it’s usually because they believe the business is worth significantly more than what they are paying, often 50% or more above the purchase price. It gives you an idea of the underlying value they saw in ZIM.

tweets.text:
People can't conceive of how fast this is coming.

Look at just the last few years of AI. It's moved faster than we can legislate, faster than we can debate, faster than any institution was built to handle. And that's the slow part. It only accelerates from here.

That's a huge elephant in the room. Shifts this big would normally go through a democratic process. People debate it, it works its way through… But that takes decades. And we don't have decades.

When the ground moves this fast and process can't keep up, governments reach for whatever knob they have. And control tends to get more centralised, not because anyone wants it, but because the democratic process simply isn't fast enough to catch what's coming.

This is the part of the Economic Singularity nobody wants to look at. It isn't just markets and machines moving to a speed we can't follow. It's our whole system of governing arriving too late.

I sat down with @PeterDiamandis and @salimismail  to go over how the next few years play out. As ever, please enjoy.
